segments.text: This piece of glass can store data for
10,000 years, and it's being kept in a
library managed by robots. I love this.
The problem with storing long-term data
on tapes and hard drives is that it's
encoded magnetically, and over time,
mechanical parts break, and the magnetic
field strength goes away. So, they might
last 5 or 10 years before needing to be
copied over. But to store data on this,
scientists use an ultraast laser to etch
the data inside the glass, permanently
encoding it in 3D pixels. This single
piece can store the equivalent of 13
years worth of music or 3,500 movies,
which might not actually sound like a
lot, but the idea is it's going to last
for way longer. So, when your great
great great great great great grandkids
are ready to read it, little robots go
and fetch the right glass card from the
library and bring it to a
computer-controlled microscope that
quickly scans the data. And then an AI
system helps decode it back into the
original information. To find out when
you might get a glass library card,
subscribe.
